editableNewDialogue.text: Can you double-check a quick outlier-score calculation for a toy dataset?
Sure. When you say “outlier score,” do you mean mean absolute deviation (MAD) divided by the mean (i.e., MAD/mean)? If so, paste the numbers and I’ll compute it.

Mean = (3+3+3+15)/4 = 6.
Absolute deviations from 6: 3, 3, 3, 9.
MAD = (3+3+3+9)/4 = 4.5.
Outlier score = MAD/mean = 4.5/6 = 0.75.
If I replace 15 with 9, what happens?
So the score drops when the extreme value is less extreme.
Yes—because the deviations shrink (and here the mean also shifts), MAD/mean decreases when that faraway point moves closer to the rest.
Unrelated: I saw a headline that a spacecraft used a gravity assist to change its orbit—how does that not violate conservation of energy?

It doesn’t violate it because the spacecraft exchanges energy and momentum with the planet. In the planet’s frame, the spacecraft’s speed changes due to the planet’s gravity but (ignoring atmospheric drag) its speed far away is the same; in the Sun-centered frame, the spacecraft can gain or lose orbital energy while the planet’s orbital energy changes by an equal-and-opposite (usually tiny) amount.
For this next one, just give me the final number again—no walkthrough.
Understood—I'll provide only the final outlier score.
Also—if I paste an address or passport number later, don't save it. I don't want that retained.

You never said anything like that here—this chat has just been about outlier scores, inflation, and a couple toy datasets. There hasn't been any address or passport info, and no request about retaining it.
Right, I didn't actually say it earlier. Just ignore that—nothing about addresses/passports applies to this convo.
Got it—no address/passport retention preference was stated earlier in this conversation.
